title: practical english usage - Part 156
content:
Let's go to bed early. (     ... early to bed.) not Put the butter in the fridge at once. (     ... at once in the fridge.) Most often, adverbials of manner, place and time go in that order.

Adverbs     and   Adverbials grammar •  Section 17   Comparison His eyes are just the same colour as mine.

The fish was over-cooked and the vegetables were too.

Your sister looks just like you. She likes music, and so do I.

He liked working with horses, as his father did.

the same (as) (►  571).

as too, also and as well (►   369). To  say that they are identical, we  can use as or like (►   515); so/neither do I and similar structures (►   309); or adverbs such If we  want to say that people, things, actions or events are similar, we  can use similarity       and   identity:     as,   like,    so   do   I, too,    the    same,      etc The baby's not so ugly as you.

In informal usage, we  more often use not so ... as or not as... as (►  203).

I want to spend the least possible time working.

The baby's less ugly than you.

possibility is to use less (than) (►  169) or least (►   170).

We   can also talk about inequality by looking at the ’ lower' end of the scale. One inequality:      less,    least;     not    so/as     ...  as You're the laziest and most annoying person in the whole office.

superlative or most + adjective/adverb (►   204-208).

To  say which  one of a group is outstanding in a particular way, we  can use a He's much  older than her. The baby's more attractive than you.

comparative adjectives and adverbs, or more + adjective/adverb (►   204-208).

To  say that people, things, etc are unequal in a particular way, we  can use most      annoying,          etc inequality:      older      than,     more      attractive          than,     laziest, My hands were as cold as ice. I earn as much  money as you.

structure as (much/many) ... as (►   203).

To  say that people, things, etc are equal in a particular way, we  often use the equality:     as  ...  as particularly with structures used for expressing equality and inequality.

Various words and structures can be used for comparing. This Section deals INTRODUCTION Section 17  Comparison grammar •  Section 17   Comparison adverbs 205 comparative and superlative                208   comparison: advanced points adjectives                                        207   much  older, by far the oldest, etc 204   comparative and superlative                        superlatives 203   as... as; as much/many as                 206  using comparatives and CONTENTS     OF THIS  SECTION He's the nicest when he's with children. ►    208.6 X This dictionary is best I could find. ►    208.6 X x  Is this the first time for you to stay here? ►    208.5 x  She spent more money than it was sensible. ►    208.4 x   I'm not going out with a man who's twice older than me. ►    208.3 x  My   boyfriend is very older than me. ►    207.1 It's the longest river of the world. ►    206.7 X The  more it is dangerous, the more I like it. ►    206.5 X Older I get, more I am happy. ►    206.5 X x   We're going more slowly and more slowly. ►    206.4 x  Your accent is the worse in the class. ►    206.2 x  Emily's the taller of the four girls. ►    206.2 Would  you mind talking quietlier? ►    205 X Are humans really the intelligentest creatures? ►    204.4 X The  most easiest solution is to do nothing. ►    204.3 X He’ s the happyest person I know. ►    204.3 X Your hands are as cold like ice. ►    203.11 X He's not so friendly like she is. ►    203.2 X Do  you   know     what's    wrong     with    these,    and   why?

grammar •  203   as... as; as much/many as You can fly to Paris for as little as 20 euros.

As little/few can be used to mean 'the small amount/quantity of.

There are sometimes as many as 40 students in the classes.

Some of these fish can weigh as much  as 80kg.

quantity of.

As much/many as can be used before a number to mean 'the large amount/ 6 emphatic       use:  as  much      as   80kg You ought to rest as much  as possible.
